channel.title: NYT > Arts
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Saudis Withdraw Offer of Millions to Metropolitan Opera
item.description: The arrangement would have brought up to $200 million to the Metropolitan Opera, which has suffered a series of financial setbacks.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nagourney
item.media:description: Opening night at the Metropolitan Opera in September.

item.title: Anne Hathaway, Queen of Effort, Is Finally Ready to Vibe
item.description: Known best for tightly wound characters in generational hit films, the actress is a tortured pop star in “Mother Mary” and returns to playful form in “The Devil Wears Prada 2.”
item.dc:creator: Joe Coscarelli, Jon Caramanica and Thea Traff
item.media:credit: Thea Traff for The New York Times

item.title: Controversies and Production Issues Around ‘Michael,’ the Jackson Biopic, Explained
item.description: Reshoots, reluctant studios and family holdouts: the production faced many issues. But now the box office is expected to be huge.
item.dc:creator: Brooks Barnes
item.media:description: Jaafar Jackson as Michael Jackson, his uncle, in the new biopic “Michael.”

item.title: How a Museum Built Its Buzz: Popcorn, Puppets and Free Tickets
item.description: The Museum of the Moving Image in Queens, long a home for cinephiles, doubled attendance by repositioning itself as a community hub.
item.dc:creator: Melena Ryzik and Gus Aronson

item.title: Is 1990s Alternative Rock the New Country?
item.description: Stagecoach Festival started out as the “country Coachella,” but has been morphing into a new home for ’90s rock bands slinging angst and guitar music.
item.dc:creator: David Malitz
item.media:description: Counting Crows in 1994. The band, led by Adam Duritz (bottom center), will perform at Stagecoach in California this weekend alongside other ’90s mainstays and a host of country acts.

item.title: ‘Beaches’ Review: A Classic Weepie Dries Its Tears
item.description: A new musical version of the 1980s tear-jerker comes to Broadway, but the production is too muddled to make an emotional impact.
item.dc:creator: Laura Collins-Hughes
item.media:credit: Rachel Papo for The New York Times
item.media:description: Kelli Barrett, left, and Jessica Vosk as the besties Bertie and Cee Cee in “Beaches” at the Majestic Theater in Manhattan.

item.title: Michael Tilson Thomas, Celebrated American Conductor, Dies at 81
item.description: A galvanizing force in classical music as a conductor, composer, pianist and evangelist, he spent 25 years as music director of the San Francisco Symphony.
item.dc:creator: Anthony Tommasini
item.media:credit: Hiroyuki Ito for The New York Times
item.media:description: Experimentation and ambitious projects marked Mr. Thomas’s years at the San Francisco Symphony. He conducted the orchestra in a Stravinsky program at Carnegie Hall in 2018.

item.title: ‘I Swear’ Review: Surviving and Thriving Against the Odds
item.description: The British actor Robert Aramayo rises above the clichés in a biographical drama about a man living with Tourette’s syndrome.
item.dc:creator: Manohla Dargis

item.title: ‘Omaha’ Review: Right Road, Wrong Destination
item.description: Great performances can’t quite save a delicate family road drama with a baffling ending.
item.dc:creator: Alissa Wilkinson

item.title: Meet the Turner Prize 2026 Nominees
item.description: This year’s shortlist for the major British art award includes an artist who stages spoken-word performances and another who makes art using oil company merch bought off eBay.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall

item.title: Stream These Movies and TV Shows Before They Leave Netflix in May
item.description: Dozens of titles leave the streaming service for U.S. subscribers every month. Here’s a roundup of the best ones leaving soon.
item.dc:creator: Jason Bailey

item.title: The Nerve Center of This Art Fair Isn’t Painting. It’s Couture.
item.description: The Independent fair will push boundaries, featuring designs by Rei Kawakubo and Comme des Garçons that blur the line between fashion and sculpture.
item.dc:creator: Tim F. Schneider

item.title: Inside LACMA’s Eye-Popping New Home, How Do You Find the Art?
item.description: Our critic calls the David Geffen Galleries “a beacon of glam with brains.” As a space to show art, it has problems. The Latino art is a revelation (if you can navigate the maze).
item.dc:creator: Holland Cotter and Jennelle Fong
item.category: Los Angeles County Museum of Art
item.media:credit: Jennelle Fong for The New York Times

item.title: Judge Dismisses Sean ‘Diddy’ Combs’s $100 Million Defamation Suit Against NBC
item.description: The music mogul, now imprisoned, had sued after NBCUniversal’s streaming service, Peacock aired the documentary “Diddy: The Making of a Bad Boy” last year.
item.dc:creator: Julia Jacobs
item.media:description: Sean Combs said an NBCUniversal documentary had defamed him, but a judge in New York ruled that his reputation had already been damaged by his criminal indictment and other matters.

item.title: Jimmy Kimmel Roasts Trump at a Mock White House Correspondents’ Dinner
item.description: The late night host took “a page from the Kid Rock alternative halftime show,” which aired during the Super Bowl, in offering his own “all-American” version of the Washington tradition.
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)

item.title: Review: ‘The Rocky Horror Show’ Revival Gives Us What We Want
item.description: Sam Pinkleton’s new revival at Studio 54 gives us the big gay mayhem we want while also maintaining some order via Rachel Dratch’s droll Narrator.
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw and Sara Krulwich

item.title: ‘Apex’ Review: Taron Egerton on the Hunt for Charlize Theron
item.description: Taron Egerton descends into full-tilt madness as a killer hunting Charlize Theron in this Netflix thriller set in southeastern Australia.
item.dc:creator: Brandon Yu

item.title: How Shikeith Brings Pittsburgh’s Black History to the Surface
item.description: With Project Blue Space, the sculptor and image maker Shikeith brings the city’s Black history to the surface.
item.dc:creator: Rose Courteau

item.title: Darrell Sheets, Auction Bidder Who Starred on ‘Storage Wars,’ Dies at 67
item.description: He appeared in 13 seasons of the long-running reality television show about storage-unit auctions. He later opened an antique shop in Arizona.
item.dc:creator: Jin Yu Young
item.media:description: Darrell Sheets in 2013. He appeared in 163 episodes of the A&E reality series “Storage Wars.”

item.title: Ruth Slenczynska, 101, Dies; Piano Prodigy Overcame Father’s Abuse
item.description: Treated brutally, she went from celebrated child star to 15-year-old “burned-out candle.” Against the odds, she later resumed performing.
item.dc:creator: Margalit Fox
item.media:credit: James J. Kriegsmann, via Bettmann Archive/Getty Images
item.media:description: Ruth Slenczynska in 1957. Her story has been cited as a textbook example of the toxic consequences that can result when a controlling parent and a gifted child collide.

item.title: After Cesar Chavez’s Fall, What to Do With the Art That Honored Him?
item.description: Artists who created public depictions of the civil rights icon Cesar Chavez have had to revisit their works after accusations emerged of Mr. Chavez’s sexual abuse of girls in the movement.
item.dc:creator: Tim Arango and Ariana Drehsler
item.media:credit: Ariana Drehsler for The New York Times

item.title: ‘Two Women’ Review: An Erotic Protest Against Monogamy
item.description: A pair of neighbors start sleeping with local handymen in this featherweight sex comedy, set in Montreal.
item.dc:creator: Natalia Winkelman

item.title: ‘Two Seasons, Two Strangers’ Review: Possibilities of a Blank Page
item.description: In this film of structural surprises, based on two works by the late manga artist Yoshiharu Tsuge, lives converge on the beach and in a wintry village.
item.dc:creator: Ben Kenigsberg

item.title: ‘Over Your Dead Body’ Review: Unholy Matrimony
item.description: Jason Segel and Samara Weaving star in this gory home-invasion comedy directed by one of the members of the Lonely Island, Jorma Taccone.
item.dc:creator: Beatrice Loayza
item.media:description: Samara Weaving, left, and Jason Segel in “Over Your Dead Body.”

item.title: ‘Fuze’ Review: Dishonor Among Thieves
item.description: A high-wattage cast led by Aaron Taylor-Johnson and Sam Worthington helps smooth this amiably absurd heist thriller.
item.dc:creator: Jeannette Catsoulis

item.title: ‘Desert Warrior’ Review: An Extra-Lavish Epic From Saudi Arabia
item.description: An action-adventure film set in seventh-century Arabia features many fighting men, but the warrior of the title turns out to be the British-Saudi actress Aiysha Hart.
item.dc:creator: Glenn Kenny

item.title: These Literary Thrillers Explore Hollywood’s Dark Side
item.description: The best-selling author Kelly Yang recommends mysteries set in Tinseltown, from the down and dirty to the deliciously dishy.
item.dc:creator: Kelly Yang

item.title: Stephen Colbert Sends Jimmy Kimmel a Box of ‘Iran War Jokes’
item.description: Colbert said he was “beginning to think this war might not be over before Memorial Day,” when “The Late Show” will have ended its run.
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: Late Show with Stephen Colbert (TV Program)
Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)
The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)

item.title: Joy Harmon, Car-Washing Temptress in ‘Cool Hand Luke,’ Dies at 87
item.description: All it took was a bucket of soapy water and a slinky housedress for her to achieve movie immortality in the 1967 Paul Newman classic.
item.dc:creator: Alex Williams

item.title: Alan Osmond, Who Led His Brothers in Song, Dies at 76
item.description: He was the eldest original member of the Osmonds, a family singing group that hit it big in the 1970s with songs like “One Bad Apple.”
item.dc:creator: Richard Sandomir
item.media:description: Alan Osmond onstage with his brothers in 1973. They began performing together in the 1950s and hit their peak in the early ’70s.

item.title: Nathalie Baye, Versatile and Admired French Actress, Dies at 77
item.description: In films like “Day for Night,” “La Balance” and “The Return of Martin Guerre,” she was adept at portraying sensual, reflective or excitable characters.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nossiter
item.media:description: Nathalie Baye in 1986. President Emmanuel Macron of France saluted the “singular path she took in our French cinema — by the side of great directors, never abandoning a popular touch.”

item.title: D4vd Murder Case: Celeste Rivas Hernandez’s Cause of Death Is Revealed
item.description: The Los Angeles police sought to keep the findings secret for months as they investigated the singer D4vd, who was charged this week with the murder of the teenager.
item.dc:creator: Matt Stevens
item.media:description: The severely decomposed remains of Celeste Rivas Hernandez were found in September, a day after what would have been her 15th birthday.

item.title: The Good List: 6 Things to Add Delight to Your Day
item.description: Mapping happiness, Monday dinners and the rise of the scapebear.
item.dc:creator: Melissa Kirsch

item.title: Review: At the Met, a Polished and Polite ‘Onegin’ Returns
item.description: A strong cast led by the soprano Asmik Grigorian lifts Deborah Warner’s production, which never quite convinces.
item.dc:creator: Arya Roshanian

item.title: ‘Half Man’ Review: Richard Gadd’s Bloody ‘Baby Reindeer’ Follow-Up
item.description: The creator of “Baby Reindeer” is back with more anger, repression and violence (and jokes) in a new series for HBO.
item.dc:creator: Mike Hale
item.media:description: Richard Gadd, left, and Jamie Bell play tortured men sharing a tortured relationship in “Half Man,” Gadd’s follow-up to “Baby Reindeer.”

item.title: Clowning Around With Fascists, in ‘The Resistible Rise of Arturo Ui’
item.description: Mark Gatiss plays a Charlie Chaplin-like dictator in a timely Royal Shakespeare Company revival.
item.dc:creator: Houman Barekat
item.media:description: Mark Gatiss, right, as the title character in “The Resistible Rise of Arturo Ui” at the Swan Theater in Stratford-upon-Avon.

item.title: In Central Java, an Eco-Resort Aims to Build Sustainability Through Creativity
item.description: When an Indonesian and Australian couple bought Yabbiekayu, their first visitors were foreigners. Now, they aim to be part of a “cultural awakening” in the region.
item.dc:creator: Nina Siegal
item.media:credit: Ulet Ifansasti for The New York Times
item.media:description: A participant during an eco-print workshop at the festival.

item.title: Dutch Panel Designs Plan to Deal With ‘Orphaned’ Nazi-Looted Art
item.description: The panel says a new Jewish foundation, not the government, should take over a massive collection of artworks whose owners have yet to be found.
item.dc:creator: Nina Siegal

item.title: Imagining the Manosphere as a Kinder, Gentler Place
item.description: Two art exhibitions examining hypermasculine online content and its impact argue that sensitivity and vulnerability are also manly virtues.
item.dc:creator: Nina Siegal

item.title: Vilma Jää’s Voice Is Unlike Anything in Opera
item.description: Vilma Jää, a Finnish folk and pop singer, is bringing her eerie and affecting traditional music to the Metropolitan Opera.
item.dc:creator: Joshua Barone and George Etheredge

item.title: 4 Takeaways From ‘Lorne,’ the Movie About Lorne Michaels and ‘S.N.L.’
item.description: The documentary offers a rare behind-the-scenes look at a producer even his cast members say is hard to know.
item.dc:creator: Melena Ryzik
item.media:description: Lorne Michaels in a scene from the new documentary about him.

item.title: École des Sables, Africa’s Premier Dance School, Faces a Precarious Future
item.description: The École des Sables has established itself as Africa’s premier dance-training hub. Yet money concerns, and a new port nearby, make its future precarious.
item.dc:creator: Laura Cappelle and Imane Djamil
item.media:credit: Imane Djamil for The New York Times

item.title: Krysten Ritter Returns as Jessica Jones in “Daredevil: Born Again”
item.description: More than six years after “Jessica Jones” came to an end, the actress returns to the role in the new season of “Daredevil: Born Again.”
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Abrams and Magdalena Wosinska
item.media:credit: Magdalena Wosinska for The New York Times
item.media:description: The plans for Ritter’s return as Jessica Jones had been in the works for years.

item.title: Michael Kosta Introduces Former Labor Secretary Lori Chavez-DeRemer
item.description: “It’s always the ones you never heard of,” the late night host said on Tuesday’s “The Daily Show.”
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: Late Show with Stephen Colbert (TV Program)
Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)
The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)

item.title: Dave Mason, Songwriter and a Co-Founder of Traffic, Dies at 79
item.description: An English singer and guitarist, he wrote classic-rock mainstays like “Feelin’ Alright?” and “Only You Know and I Know.” He also had a successful solo career, singing “We Just Disagree.”
item.dc:creator: Jim Farber
item.media:description: Dave Mason performing in 1980. As a solo artist, he released three gold albums, including his solo debut in 1970, “Alone Together.”

item.title: Olafur Eliasson Uses Art and Sound to Raise Climate Awareness in Utah
item.description: The Danish Icelandic artist reflects on his childhood and how it influenced and empowered his art.
item.dc:creator: Farah Nayeri

item.title: The Vienna Climate Biennale Contrasts Chaos with Hope
item.description: In a world marked by ecological, social and political crises, the art festival aims to get people talking, thinking and ready to imagine a better world.
item.dc:creator: Keridwen Cornelius

item.title: A Mural by the Painter of ‘American Gothic’ Gets New Life
item.description: Grant Wood’s 1926 “Corn Room” has been restored and will anchor an exhibition celebrating its centennial at the Sioux City Art Center in Iowa.
item.dc:creator: Alex V. Cipolle
item.media:credit: Jenn Ackerman for The New York Times
item.media:description: From the left, Rita Berg, Tailor Choquette, Alexa Beller, and Kristy Jeffcoat working on Grant Wood’s “Corn Room” mural at the Midwest Art Conservation Center in Minneapolis.

item.title: An Installation in Nature has Climate Lessons for Humans
item.description: With lichen, mechanical artwork and a barrel of snowflakes, “Climate Clock” will wind along a trail through the forests across Oulu, Finland.
item.dc:creator: David Belcher

item.title: The Haas Brothers’ Creative Creations Are on Tour
item.description: A midcareer survey of the work by the twins Simon Haas and Nikolai Haas is touring the country.
item.dc:creator: Ted Loos
item.media:credit: Stella Kalinina for The New York Times

item.title: Review: In ‘The Balusters,’ Neighborly Dysfunction Is on the Agenda
item.description: David Lindsay-Abaire’s comedy about a wealthy homeowners association thrown into disarray makes a case for the same social compact it skewers.
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw
item.media:description: Clockwise from top left, Ricardo Chavira, Carl Clemons-Hopkins, Richard Thomas, Marylouise Burke, Jeena Yi, Kayli Carter and Anika Noni Rose in “The Balusters,” at the Samuel J. Friedman in Manhattan.

item.title: With $116 Million Gift, National Gallery Will Send Its Art Around Nation
item.description: The large contribution from the billionaire collector Mitchell P. Rales is enabling long-term loans to smaller museums in perpetuity.
item.dc:creator: Robin Pogrebin

item.title: Andrew Hacker, Author Who Challenged Conventional Thinking, Dies at 96
item.description: In a host of books and articles as a political scientist, he attacked received ideas on the battle of the sexes, the usefulness of high school math and other subjects.
item.dc:creator: Robert D. McFadden
item.category: Queens College, City University of New York
Mismatch: The Growing Gulf Between Women and Men (Book)
item.media:credit: Richard Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Andrew Hacker at Queens College in 2004. Whatever the subject, Mr. Hacker’s ideas were usually iconoclastic and irritating.

item.title: In ‘What Happened Was …,’ Cecily Strong and Corey Stoll Cook Up a Storm
item.description: Cecily Strong and Corey Stoll star in this two-hander about connecting over a meal that becomes much deeper than two colleagues socializing out-of-office.
item.dc:creator: Tim Teeman
item.media:description: Cecily Strong and Corey Stoll in “What Happened Was …” at the Minetta Lane Theater in Manhattan.

item.title: ‘Michael’ Review: A Jackson Biopic Leaves Too Much Unsaid
item.description: A biographical film produced by the estate of Michael Jackson flattens its subject to scrub his reputation.
item.dc:creator: Alissa Wilkinson

item.title: 7 New Songs You Should Hear Now
item.description: Nine Inch Nails’ LP with Boys Noize, Raye’s collaboration with Al Green, the latest from a Black Midi member and more.
item.dc:creator: Lindsay Zoladz
item.media:description: Nine Inch Noize onstage at Coachella last weekend.

item.title: Nate Smith Is Bringing a Big-Tent Approach to the Newport Jazz Festival
item.description: The Grammy-winning drummer — and the event’s latest artistic director — uses new-school methods to maintain the genre’s essential tradition: live collaboration.
item.dc:creator: Elena Bergeron
item.media:credit: Riley Goodman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Nate Smith said one of his goals as artistic director of the Newport Jazz Festival is to foster personal connections among musicians and listeners.

item.title: Smithsonian American Art Museum Names a New Director
item.description: Lynda Roscoe Hartigan is returning to the museum as its leader after previously serving as its chief curator.
item.dc:creator: Robin Pogrebin

item.title: ‘My Brother the Minotaur’ Is a Visually Stunning Animated Series
item.description: The Apple TV series leans into the natural splendor of its wild, rustic setting, with a painterliness that recalls Celtic and medieval traditions.
item.dc:creator: Connie Chang
item.media:description: Pooka (voiced by Paul Kaye) and Lorcan (Ely Solan) in "My Brother the Minotaur," from the studio behind “The Secret of Kells.”

item.title: Gregory Spears: Composer Who Loops Musical Time and History
item.description: Gregory Spears is bringing his uncanny sense of genre and eras of music to the “Sleeping Beauty” story with his new opera, “Sleepers Awake.”
item.dc:creator: Joshua Barone and Michelle Gustafson
item.category: Kennedy, John F, Center for the Performing Arts
item.media:description: The composer Gregory Spears in Philadelphia.
